# Claude Code settings

> Configure Claude Code with global and project-level settings, and environment variables.

Claude Code offers a variety of settings to configure its behavior to meet your needs. You can configure Claude Code by running the `/config` command when using the interactive REPL, which opens a tabbed Settings interface where you can view status information and modify configuration options.

## Configuration scopes

Claude Code uses a **scope system** to determine where configurations apply and who they're shared with. Understanding scopes helps you decide how to configure Claude Code for personal use, team collaboration, or enterprise deployment.

### Available scopes

| Scope       | Location                                                                           | Who it affects                       | Shared with team?      |
| :---------- | :--------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | :----------------------------------- | :--------------------- |
| **Managed** | Server-managed settings, plist / registry, or system-level `managed-settings.json` | All users on the machine             | Yes (deployed by IT)   |
| **User**    | `~/.claude/` directory                                                             | You, across all projects             | No                     |
| **Project** | `.claude/` in repository                                                           | All collaborators on this repository | Yes (committed to git) |
| **Local**   | `.claude/settings.local.json`                                                      | You, in this repository only         | No (gitignored)        |

### When to use each scope

**Managed scope** is for:

* Security policies that must be enforced organization-wide

5	# Claude Code settings
6	
7	> Configure Claude Code with global and project-level settings, and environment variables.
8	
9	Claude Code offers a variety of settings to configure its behavior to meet your needs. You can configure Claude Code by running the `/config` command when using the interactive REPL, which opens a tabbed Settings interface where you can view status information and modify configuration options.
10	
11	## Configuration scopes
12	
13	Claude Code uses a **scope system** to determine where configurations apply and who they're shared with. Understanding scopes helps you decide how to configure Claude Code for personal use, team collaboration, or enterprise deployment.
14	
15	### Available scopes
16	
17	| Scope       | Location                                                                           | Who it affects                       | Shared with team?      |
18	| :---------- | :--------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | :----------------------------------- | :--------------------- |
19	| **Managed** | Server-managed settings, plist / registry, or system-level `managed-settings.json` | All users on the machine             | Yes (deployed by IT)   |
20	| **User**    | `~/.claude/` directory                                                             | You, across all projects             | No                     |
21	| **Project** | `.claude/` in repository                                                           | All collaborators on this repository | Yes (committed to git) |
22	| **Local**   | `.claude/settings.local.json`                                                      | You, in this repository only         | No (gitignored)        |
23	
24	### When to use each scope
25	
26	**Managed scope** is for:
27	
28	* Security policies that must be enforced organization-wide
29	* Compliance requirements that can't be overridden
30	* Standardized configurations deployed by IT/DevOps
31	
32	**User scope** is best for:
33	
34	* Personal preferences you want everywhere (themes, editor settings)
35	* Tools and plugins you use across all projects
36	* API keys and authentication (stored securely)
37	
38	**Project scope** is best for:
39	
40	* Team-shared settings (permissions, hooks, MCP servers)
41	* Plugins the whole team should have
42	* Standardizing tooling across collaborators
43	
44	**Local scope** is best for:
45	
46	* Personal overrides for a specific project
47	* Testing configurations before sharing with the team
48	* Machine-specific settings that won't work for others
49	
50	### How scopes interact
51	
52	When the same setting is configured in multiple scopes, more specific scopes take precedence:
53	
54	1. **Managed** (highest) - can't be overridden by anything
55	2. **Command line arguments** - temporary session overrides
56	3. **Local** - overrides project and user settings
57	4. **Project** - overrides user settings
58	5. **User** (lowest) - applies when nothing else specifies the setting
59	
60	For example, if a permission is allowed in user settings but denied in project settings, the project setting takes precedence and the permission is blocked.
61	
62	### What uses scopes
63	
64	Scopes apply to many Claude Code features:
65	
66	| Feature         | User location             | Project location                   | Local location                 |
67	| :-------------- | :------------------------ | :--------------------------------- | :----------------------------- |
68	| **Settings**    | `~/.claude/settings.json` | `.claude/settings.json`            | `.claude/settings.local.json`  |
69	| **Subagents**   | `~/.claude/agents/`       | `.claude/agents/`                  | None                           |
70	| **MCP servers** | `~/.claude.json`          | `.mcp.json`                        | `~/.claude.json` (per-project) |
71	| **Plugins**     | `~/.claude/settings.json` | `.claude/settings.json`            | `.claude/settings.local.json`  |
72	| **CLAUDE.md**   | `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`     | `CLAUDE.md` or `.claude/CLAUDE.md` | `CLAUDE.local.md`              |
73	
74	***
75	
76	## Settings files
77	
78	The `settings.json` file is the official mechanism for configuring Claude
79	Code through hierarchical settings:
80	
81	* **User settings** are defined in `~/.claude/settings.json` and apply to all
82	  projects.
83	* **Project settings** are saved in your project directory:
84	  * `.claude/settings.json` for settings that are checked into source control and shared with your team
85	  * `.claude/settings.local.json` for settings that are not checked in, useful for personal preferences and experimentation. Claude Code will configure git to ignore `.claude/settings.local.json` when it is created.
86	* **Managed settings**: For organizations that need centralized control, Claude Code supports multiple delivery mechanisms for managed settings. All use the same JSON format and cannot be overridden by user or project settings:
87	
88	  * **Server-managed settings**: delivered from Anthropic's servers via the Claude.ai admin console. See [server-managed settings](/en/server-managed-settings).
89	  * **MDM/OS-level policies**: delivered through native device management on macOS and Windows:
90	    * macOS: `com.anthropic.claudecode` managed preferences domain (deployed via configuration profiles in Jamf, Iru (Kandji), or other MDM tools)
91	    * Windows: `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\ClaudeCode` registry key with a `Settings` value (REG\_SZ or REG\_EXPAND\_SZ) containing JSON (deployed via Group Policy or Intune)
92	    * Windows (user-level): `HKCU\SOFTWARE\Policies\ClaudeCode` (lowest policy priority, only used when no admin-level source exists)
93	  * **File-based**: `managed-settings.json` and `managed-mcp.json` deployed to system directories:
94	
95	    * macOS: `/Library/Application Support/ClaudeCode/`
96	    * Linux and WSL: `/etc/claude-code/`
97	    * Windows: `C:\Program Files\ClaudeCode\`
98	
99	    <Warning>
100	      The legacy Windows path `C:\ProgramData\ClaudeCode\managed-settings.json` is no longer supported as of v2.1.75. Administrators who deployed settings to that location must migrate files to `C:\Program Files\ClaudeCode\managed-settings.json`.
101	    </Warning>
102	
103	    File-based managed settings also support a drop-in directory at `managed-settings.d/` in the same system directory alongside `managed-settings.json`. This lets separate teams deploy independent policy fragments without coordinating edits to a single file.
104	
105	    Following the systemd convention, `managed-settings.json` is merged first as the base, then all `*.json` files in the drop-in directory are sorted alphabetically and merged on top. Later files override earlier ones for scalar values; arrays are concatenated and de-duplicated; objects are deep-merged. Hidden files starting with `.` are ignored.
106	
107	    Use numeric prefixes to control merge order, for example `10-telemetry.json` and `20-security.json`.
108	
109	  See [managed settings](/en/permissions#managed-only-settings) and [Managed MCP configuration](/en/mcp#managed-mcp-configuration) for details.
110	
111	  This [repository](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/tree/main/examples/mdm) includes starter deployment templates for Jamf, Iru (Kandji), Intune, and Group Policy. Use these as starting points and adjust them to fit your needs.
112	
113	  <Note>
114	    Managed deployments can also restrict **plugin marketplace additions** using
115	    `strictKnownMarketplaces`. For more information, see [Managed marketplace restrictions](/en/plugin-marketplaces#managed-marketplace-restrictions).
116	  </Note>
117	* **Other configuration** is stored in `~/.claude.json`. This file contains your preferences (theme, notification settings, editor mode), OAuth session, [MCP server](/en/mcp) configurations for user and local scopes, per-project state (allowed tools, trust settings), and various caches. Project-scoped MCP servers are stored separately in `.mcp.json`.
118	
119	<Note>
120	  Claude Code automatically creates timestamped backups of configuration files and retains the five most recent backups to prevent data loss.
121	</Note>
122	
123	```JSON Example settings.json theme={null}
124	{
125	  "$schema": "https://json.schemastore.org/claude-code-settings.json",
126	  "permissions": {
127	    "allow": [
128	      "Bash(npm run lint)",
129	      "Bash(npm run test *)",
130	      "Read(~/.zshrc)"
131	    ],
132	    "deny": [
133	      "Bash(curl *)",
134	      "Read(./.env)",
135	      "Read(./.env.*)",
136	      "Read(./secrets/**)"
137	    ]
138	  },
139	  "env": {
140	    "CLAUDE_CODE_ENABLE_TELEMETRY": "1",
141	    "OTEL_METRICS_EXPORTER": "otlp"
142	  },
143	  "companyAnnouncements": [
144	    "Welcome to Acme Corp! Review our code guidelines at docs.acme.com",
145	    "Reminder: Code reviews required for all PRs",
146	    "New security policy in effect"
147	  ]
148	}
149	```
150	
151	The `$schema` line in the example above points to the [official JSON schema](https://json.schemastore.org/claude-code-settings.json) for Claude Code settings. Adding it to your `settings.json` enables autocomplete and inline validation in VS Code, Cursor, and any other editor that supports JSON schema validation.
152	
153	The published schema is updated periodically and may not include settings added in the most recent CLI releases, so a validation warning on a recently documented field does not necessarily mean your configuration is invalid.

264	### Permission rule syntax
265	
266	Permission rules follow the format `Tool` or `Tool(specifier)`. Rules are evaluated in order: deny rules first, then ask, then allow. The first matching rule wins.
267	
268	Quick examples:
269	
270	| Rule                           | Effect                                   |
271	| :----------------------------- | :--------------------------------------- |
272	| `Bash`                         | Matches all Bash commands                |
273	| `Bash(npm run *)`              | Matches commands starting with `npm run` |
274	| `Read(./.env)`                 | Matches reading the `.env` file          |
275	| `WebFetch(domain:example.com)` | Matches fetch requests to example.com    |
276	
277	For the complete rule syntax reference, including wildcard behavior, tool-specific patterns for Read, Edit, WebFetch, MCP, and Agent rules, and security limitations of Bash patterns, see [Permission rule syntax](/en/permissions#permission-rule-syntax).

307	#### Sandbox path prefixes
308	
309	Paths in `filesystem.allowWrite`, `filesystem.denyWrite`, `filesystem.denyRead`, and `filesystem.allowRead` support these prefixes:
310	
311	| Prefix            | Meaning                                                                                | Example                                                                   |
312	| :---------------- | :------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | :------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
313	| `/`               | Absolute path from filesystem root                                                     | `/tmp/build` stays `/tmp/build`                                           |
314	| `~/`              | Relative to home directory                                                             | `~/.kube` becomes `$HOME/.kube`                                           |
315	| `./` or no prefix | Relative to the project root for project settings, or to `~/.claude` for user settings | `./output` in `.claude/settings.json` resolves to `<project-root>/output` |
316	
317	The older `//path` prefix for absolute paths still works. If you previously used single-slash `/path` expecting project-relative resolution, switch to `./path`. This syntax differs from [Read and Edit permission rules](/en/permissions#read-and-edit), which use `//path` for absolute and `/path` for project-relative. Sandbox filesystem paths use standard conventions: `/tmp/build` is an absolute path.
318	
319	**Configuration example:**
320	
321	```json theme={null}
322	{
323	  "sandbox": {
324	    "enabled": true,
325	    "autoAllowBashIfSandboxed": true,
326	    "excludedCommands": ["docker *"],
327	    "filesystem": {
328	      "allowWrite": ["/tmp/build", "~/.kube"],
329	      "denyRead": ["~/.aws/credentials"]
330	    },
331	    "network": {
332	      "allowedDomains": ["github.com", "*.npmjs.org", "registry.yarnpkg.com"],
333	      "deniedDomains": ["uploads.github.com"],
334	      "allowUnixSockets": [
335	        "/var/run/docker.sock"
336	      ],
337	      "allowLocalBinding": true
338	    }
339	  }
340	}
341	```
342	
343	**Filesystem and network restrictions** can be configured in two ways that are merged together:
344	
345	* **`sandbox.filesystem` settings** (shown above): Control paths at the OS-level sandbox boundary. These restrictions apply to all subprocess commands (e.g., `kubectl`, `terraform`, `npm`), not just Claude's file tools.
346	* **Permission rules**: Use `Edit` allow/deny rules to control Claude's file tool access, `Read` deny rules to block reads, and `WebFetch` allow/deny rules to control network domains. Paths from these rules are also merged into the sandbox configuration.
347	
348	### Attribution settings
349	
350	Claude Code adds attribution to git commits and pull requests. These are configured separately:
351	
352	* Commits use [git trailers](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-interpret-trailers) (like `Co-Authored-By`) by default,  which can be customized or disabled
353	* Pull request descriptions are plain text
354	
355	| Keys     | Description                                                                                |
356	| :------- | :----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
357	| `commit` | Attribution for git commits, including any trailers. Empty string hides commit attribution |
358	| `pr`     | Attribution for pull request descriptions. Empty string hides pull request attribution     |
359	
360	**Default commit attribution:**
361	
362	```text theme={null}
363	🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
364	
365	   Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
366	```
367	
368	**Default pull request attribution:**
369	
370	```text theme={null}
371	🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
372	```
373	
374	**Example:**
375	
376	```json theme={null}
377	{
378	  "attribution": {
379	    "commit": "Generated with AI\n\nCo-Authored-By: AI <ai@example.com>",
380	    "pr": ""
381	  }
382	}
383	```
384	
385	<Note>
386	  The `attribution` setting takes precedence over the deprecated `includeCoAuthoredBy` setting. To hide all attribution, set `commit` and `pr` to empty strings.
387	</Note>

403	
404	```json theme={null}
405	{"query": "src/comp"}
406	```

408	Output newline-separated file paths to stdout (currently limited to 15):
409	
410	```text theme={null}
411	src/components/Button.tsx
412	src/components/Modal.tsx
413	src/components/Form.tsx
414	```

416	**Example:**
417	
418	```bash theme={null}
419	#!/bin/bash
420	query=$(cat | jq -r '.query')
421	your-repo-file-index --query "$query" | head -20
422	```

424	### Hook configuration
425	
426	These settings control which hooks are allowed to run and what HTTP hooks can access. The `allowManagedHooksOnly` setting can only be configured in [managed settings](#settings-files). The URL and env var allowlists can be set at any settings level and merge across sources.
427	
428	**Behavior when `allowManagedHooksOnly` is `true`:**
429	
430	* Managed hooks and SDK hooks are loaded
431	* Hooks from plugins force-enabled in managed settings `enabledPlugins` are loaded. This lets administrators distribute vetted hooks through an organization marketplace while blocking everything else. Trust is granted by full `plugin@marketplace` ID, so a plugin with the same name from a different marketplace stays blocked
432	* User hooks, project hooks, and all other plugin hooks are blocked
433	
434	**Restrict HTTP hook URLs:**
435	
436	Limit which URLs HTTP hooks can target. Supports `*` as a wildcard for matching. When the array is defined, HTTP hooks targeting non-matching URLs are silently blocked.
437	
438	```json theme={null}
439	{
440	  "allowedHttpHookUrls": ["https://hooks.example.com/*", "http://localhost:*"]
441	}
442	```
443	
444	**Restrict HTTP hook environment variables:**
445	
446	Limit which environment variable names HTTP hooks can interpolate into header values. Each hook's effective `allowedEnvVars` is the intersection of its own list and this setting.
447	
448	```json theme={null}
449	{
450	  "httpHookAllowedEnvVars": ["MY_TOKEN", "HOOK_SECRET"]
451	}
452	```
453	
454	### Settings precedence
455	
456	Settings apply in order of precedence. From highest to lowest:
457	
458	1. **Managed settings** ([server-managed](/en/server-managed-settings), [MDM/OS-level policies](#configuration-scopes), or [managed settings](/en/settings#settings-files))
459	   * Policies deployed by IT through server delivery, MDM configuration profiles, registry policies, or managed settings files
460	   * Cannot be overridden by any other level, including command line arguments
461	   * Within the managed tier, precedence is: server-managed > MDM/OS-level policies > file-based (`managed-settings.d/*.json` + `managed-settings.json`) > HKCU registry (Windows only). Only one managed source is used; sources do not merge across tiers. Within the file-based tier, drop-in files and the base file are merged together.
462	
463	2. **Command line arguments**
464	   * Temporary overrides for a specific session
465	
466	3. **Local project settings** (`.claude/settings.local.json`)
467	   * Personal project-specific settings
468	
469	4. **Shared project settings** (`.claude/settings.json`)
470	   * Team-shared project settings in source control
471	
472	5. **User settings** (`~/.claude/settings.json`)
473	   * Personal global settings
474	
475	This hierarchy ensures that organizational policies are always enforced while still allowing teams and individuals to customize their experience. The same precedence applies whether you run Claude Code from the CLI, the [VS Code extension](/en/vs-code), or a [JetBrains IDE](/en/jetbrains).
476	
477	For example, if your user settings allow `Bash(npm run *)` but a project's shared settings deny it, the project setting takes precedence and the command is blocked.
478	
479	<Note>
480	  **Array settings merge across scopes.** When the same array-valued setting (such as `sandbox.filesystem.allowWrite` or `permissions.allow`) appears in multiple scopes, the arrays are **concatenated and deduplicated**, not replaced. This means lower-priority scopes can add entries without overriding those set by higher-priority scopes, and vice versa. For example, if managed settings set `allowWrite` to `["/opt/company-tools"]` and a user adds `["~/.kube"]`, both paths are included in the final configuration.
481	</Note>
482	
483	### Verify active settings
484	
485	Run `/status` inside Claude Code to see which settings sources are active and where they come from. The output shows each configuration layer (managed, user, project) along with its origin, such as `Enterprise managed settings (remote)`, `Enterprise managed settings (plist)`, `Enterprise managed settings (HKLM)`, or `Enterprise managed settings (file)`. If a settings file contains errors, `/status` reports the issue so you can fix it.
486	
487	### Key points about the configuration system
488	
489	* **Memory files (`CLAUDE.md`)**: Contain instructions and context that Claude loads at startup
490	* **Settings files (JSON)**: Configure permissions, environment variables, and tool behavior
491	* **Skills**: Custom prompts that can be invoked with `/skill-name` or loaded by Claude automatically
492	* **MCP servers**: Extend Claude Code with additional tools and integrations
493	* **Precedence**: Higher-level configurations (Managed) override lower-level ones (User/Project)
494	* **Inheritance**: Settings are merged, with more specific settings adding to or overriding broader ones
495	
496	### System prompt
497	
498	Claude Code's internal system prompt is not published. To add custom instructions, use `CLAUDE.md` files or the `--append-system-prompt` flag.
499	
500	### Excluding sensitive files
501	
502	To prevent Claude Code from accessing files containing sensitive information like API keys, secrets, and environment files, use the `permissions.deny` setting in your `.claude/settings.json` file:
503	
504	```json theme={null}
505	{
506	  "permissions": {
507	    "deny": [
508	      "Read(./.env)",
509	      "Read(./.env.*)",
510	      "Read(./secrets/**)",
511	      "Read(./config/credentials.json)",
512	      "Read(./build)"
513	    ]
514	  }
515	}
516	```
517	
518	This replaces the deprecated `ignorePatterns` configuration. Files matching these patterns are excluded from file discovery and search results, and read operations on these files are denied.
519	
520	## Subagent configuration
521	
522	Claude Code supports custom AI subagents that can be configured at both user and project levels. These subagents are stored as Markdown files with YAML frontmatter:
523	
524	* **User subagents**: `~/.claude/agents/` - Available across all your projects
525	* **Project subagents**: `.claude/agents/` - Specific to your project and can be shared with your team
526	
527	Subagent files define specialized AI assistants with custom prompts and tool permissions. Learn more about creating and using subagents in the [subagents documentation](/en/sub-agents).
528	
529	## Plugin configuration
530	
531	Claude Code supports a plugin system that lets you extend functionality with skills, agents, hooks, and MCP servers. Plugins are distributed through marketplaces and can be configured at both user and repository levels.
532	
533	### Plugin settings
534	
535	Plugin-related settings in `settings.json`:
536	
537	```json theme={null}
538	{
539	  "enabledPlugins": {
540	    "formatter@acme-tools": true,
541	    "deployer@acme-tools": true,
542	    "analyzer@security-plugins": false
543	  },
544	  "extraKnownMarketplaces": {
545	    "acme-tools": {
546	      "source": "github",
547	      "repo": "acme-corp/claude-plugins"
548	    }
549	  }
550	}
551	```
552	
553	#### `enabledPlugins`
554	
555	Controls which plugins are enabled. Format: `"plugin-name@marketplace-name": true/false`
556	
557	**Scopes**:
558	
559	* **User settings** (`~/.claude/settings.json`): Personal plugin preferences
560	* **Project settings** (`.claude/settings.json`): Project-specific plugins shared with team
561	* **Local settings** (`.claude/settings.local.json`): Per-machine overrides (not committed)
562	* **Managed settings** (`managed-settings.json`): Organization-wide policy overrides that block installation at all scopes and hide the plugin from the marketplace
563	
564	**Example**:
565	
566	```json theme={null}
567	{
568	  "enabledPlugins": {
569	    "code-formatter@team-tools": true,
570	    "deployment-tools@team-tools": true,
571	    "experimental-features@personal": false
572	  }
573	}
574	```
575	
576	#### `extraKnownMarketplaces`
577	
578	Defines additional marketplaces that should be made available for the repository. Typically used in repository-level settings to ensure team members have access to required plugin sources.
579	
580	**When a repository includes `extraKnownMarketplaces`**:
581	
582	1. Team members are prompted to install the marketplace when they trust the folder
583	2. Team members are then prompted to install plugins from that marketplace
584	3. Users can skip unwanted marketplaces or plugins (stored in user settings)
585	4. Installation respects trust boundaries and requires explicit consent
586	
587	**Example**:
588	
589	```json theme={null}
590	{
591	  "extraKnownMarketplaces": {
592	    "acme-tools": {
593	      "source": {
594	        "source": "github",
595	        "repo": "acme-corp/claude-plugins"
596	      }
597	    },
598	    "security-plugins": {
599	      "source": {
600	        "source": "git",
601	        "url": "https://git.example.com/security/plugins.git"
602	      }
603	    }
604	  }
605	}
606	```
607	
608	**Marketplace source types**:
609	
610	* `github`: GitHub repository (uses `repo`)
611	* `git`: Any git URL (uses `url`)
612	* `directory`: Local filesystem path (uses `path`, for development only)
613	* `hostPattern`: regex pattern to match marketplace hosts (uses `hostPattern`)
614	* `settings`: inline marketplace declared directly in settings.json without a separate hosted repository (uses `name` and `plugins`)
615	
616	Use `source: 'settings'` to declare a small set of plugins inline without setting up a hosted marketplace repository. Plugins listed here must reference external sources such as GitHub or npm. You still need to enable each plugin separately in `enabledPlugins`.
617	
618	```json theme={null}
619	{
620	  "extraKnownMarketplaces": {
621	    "team-tools": {
622	      "source": {
623	        "source": "settings",
624	        "name": "team-tools",
625	        "plugins": [
626	          {
627	            "name": "code-formatter",
628	            "source": {
629	              "source": "github",
630	              "repo": "acme-corp/code-formatter"
631	            }
632	          }
633	        ]
634	      }
635	    }
636	  }
637	}
638	```
639	
640	#### `strictKnownMarketplaces`
641	
642	**Managed settings only**: Controls which plugin marketplaces users are allowed to add. This setting can only be configured in [managed settings](/en/settings#settings-files) and provides administrators with strict control over marketplace sources.
643	
644	**Managed settings file locations**:
645	
646	* **macOS**: `/Library/Application Support/ClaudeCode/managed-settings.json`
647	* **Linux and WSL**: `/etc/claude-code/managed-settings.json`
648	* **Windows**: `C:\Program Files\ClaudeCode\managed-settings.json`
649	
650	**Key characteristics**:
651	
652	* Only available in managed settings (`managed-settings.json`)
653	* Cannot be overridden by user or project settings (highest precedence)
654	* Enforced BEFORE network/filesystem operations (blocked sources never execute)
655	* Uses exact matching for source specifications (including `ref`, `path` for git sources), except `hostPattern`, which uses regex matching
656	
657	**Allowlist behavior**:
658	
659	* `undefined` (default): No restrictions - users can add any marketplace
660	* Empty array `[]`: Complete lockdown - users cannot add any new marketplaces
661	* List of sources: Users can only add marketplaces that match exactly
662	
663	**All supported source types**:
664	
665	The allowlist supports multiple marketplace source types. Most sources use exact matching, while `hostPattern` uses regex matching against the marketplace host.
666	
667	1. **GitHub repositories**:
668	
669	```json theme={null}
670	{ "source": "github", "repo": "acme-corp/approved-plugins" }
671	{ "source": "github", "repo": "acme-corp/security-tools", "ref": "v2.0" }
672	{ "source": "github", "repo": "acme-corp/plugins", "ref": "main", "path": "marketplace" }
673	```
674	
675	Fields: `repo` (required), `ref` (optional: branch/tag/SHA), `path` (optional: subdirectory)
676	
677	2. **Git repositories**:
678	
679	```json theme={null}
680	{ "source": "git", "url": "https://gitlab.example.com/tools/plugins.git" }
681	{ "source": "git", "url": "https://bitbucket.org/acme-corp/plugins.git", "ref": "production" }
682	{ "source": "git", "url": "ssh://git@git.example.com/plugins.git", "ref": "v3.1", "path": "approved" }
683	```
684	
685	Fields: `url` (required), `ref` (optional: branch/tag/SHA), `path` (optional: subdirectory)
686	
687	3. **URL-based marketplaces**:
688	
689	```json theme={null}
690	{ "source": "url", "url": "https://plugins.example.com/marketplace.json" }
691	{ "source": "url", "url": "https://cdn.example.com/marketplace.json", "headers": { "Authorization": "Bearer ${TOKEN}" } }
692	```
693	
694	Fields: `url` (required), `headers` (optional: HTTP headers for authenticated access)
695	
696	<Note>
697	  URL-based marketplaces only download the `marketplace.json` file. They do not download plugin files from the server. Plugins in URL-based marketplaces must use external sources (GitHub, npm, or git URLs) rather than relative paths. For plugins with relative paths, use a Git-based marketplace instead. See [Troubleshooting](/en/plugin-marketplaces#plugins-with-relative-paths-fail-in-url-based-marketplaces) for details.
698	</Note>
699	
700	4. **NPM packages**:
701	
702	```json theme={null}
703	{ "source": "npm", "package": "@acme-corp/claude-plugins" }
704	{ "source": "npm", "package": "@acme-corp/approved-marketplace" }
705	```
706	
707	Fields: `package` (required, supports scoped packages)
708	
709	5. **File paths**:
710	
711	```json theme={null}
712	{ "source": "file", "path": "/usr/local/share/claude/acme-marketplace.json" }
713	{ "source": "file", "path": "/opt/acme-corp/plugins/marketplace.json" }
714	```
715	
716	Fields: `path` (required: absolute path to marketplace.json file)
717	
718	6. **Directory paths**:
719	
720	```json theme={null}
721	{ "source": "directory", "path": "/usr/local/share/claude/acme-plugins" }
722	{ "source": "directory", "path": "/opt/acme-corp/approved-marketplaces" }
723	```
724	
725	Fields: `path` (required: absolute path to directory containing `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json`)
726	
727	7. **Host pattern matching**:
728	
729	```json theme={null}
730	{ "source": "hostPattern", "hostPattern": "^github\\.example\\.com$" }
731	{ "source": "hostPattern", "hostPattern": "^gitlab\\.internal\\.example\\.com$" }
732	```
733	
734	Fields: `hostPattern` (required: regex pattern to match against the marketplace host)
735	
736	Use host pattern matching when you want to allow all marketplaces from a specific host without enumerating each repository individually. This is useful for organizations with internal GitHub Enterprise or GitLab servers where developers create their own marketplaces.
737	
738	Host extraction by source type:
739	
740	* `github`: always matches against `github.com`
741	* `git`: extracts hostname from the URL (supports both HTTPS and SSH formats)
742	* `url`: extracts hostname from the URL
743	* `npm`, `file`, `directory`: not supported for host pattern matching
744	
745	**Configuration examples**:
746	
747	Example: allow specific marketplaces only:
748	
749	```json theme={null}
750	{
751	  "strictKnownMarketplaces": [
752	    {
753	      "source": "github",
754	      "repo": "acme-corp/approved-plugins"
755	    },
756	    {
757	      "source": "github",
758	      "repo": "acme-corp/security-tools",
759	      "ref": "v2.0"
760	    },
761	    {
762	      "source": "url",
763	      "url": "https://plugins.example.com/marketplace.json"
764	    },
765	    {
766	      "source": "npm",
767	      "package": "@acme-corp/compliance-plugins"
768	    }
769	  ]
770	}
771	```
772	
773	Example - Disable all marketplace additions:
774	
775	```json theme={null}
776	{
777	  "strictKnownMarketplaces": []
778	}
779	```
780	
781	Example: allow all marketplaces from an internal git server:
782	
783	```json theme={null}
784	{
785	  "strictKnownMarketplaces": [
786	    {
787	      "source": "hostPattern",
788	      "hostPattern": "^github\\.example\\.com$"
789	    }
790	  ]
791	}
792	```
793	
794	**Exact matching requirements**:
795	
796	Marketplace sources must match **exactly** for a user's addition to be allowed. For git-based sources (`github` and `git`), this includes all optional fields:
797	
798	* The `repo` or `url` must match exactly
799	* The `ref` field must match exactly (or both be undefined)
800	* The `path` field must match exactly (or both be undefined)
801	
802	Examples of sources that **do NOT match**:
803	
804	```json theme={null}
805	// These are DIFFERENT sources:
806	{ "source": "github", "repo": "acme-corp/plugins" }
807	{ "source": "github", "repo": "acme-corp/plugins", "ref": "main" }
808	
809	// These are also DIFFERENT:
810	{ "source": "github", "repo": "acme-corp/plugins", "path": "marketplace" }
811	{ "source": "github", "repo": "acme-corp/plugins" }
812	```
813	
814	**Comparison with `extraKnownMarketplaces`**:
815	
816	| Aspect                | `strictKnownMarketplaces`            | `extraKnownMarketplaces`             |
817	| --------------------- | ------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------ |
818	| **Purpose**           | Organizational policy enforcement    | Team convenience                     |
819	| **Settings file**     | `managed-settings.json` only         | Any settings file                    |
820	| **Behavior**          | Blocks non-allowlisted additions     | Auto-installs missing marketplaces   |
821	| **When enforced**     | Before network/filesystem operations | After user trust prompt              |
822	| **Can be overridden** | No (highest precedence)              | Yes (by higher precedence settings)  |
823	| **Source format**     | Direct source object                 | Named marketplace with nested source |
824	| **Use case**          | Compliance, security restrictions    | Onboarding, standardization          |
825	
826	**Format difference**:
827	
828	`strictKnownMarketplaces` uses direct source objects:
829	
830	```json theme={null}
831	{
832	  "strictKnownMarketplaces": [
833	    { "source": "github", "repo": "acme-corp/plugins" }
834	  ]
835	}
836	```
837	
838	`extraKnownMarketplaces` requires named marketplaces:
839	
840	```json theme={null}
841	{
842	  "extraKnownMarketplaces": {
843	    "acme-tools": {
844	      "source": { "source": "github", "repo": "acme-corp/plugins" }
845	    }
846	  }
847	}
848	```
849	
850	**Using both together**:
851	
852	`strictKnownMarketplaces` is a policy gate: it controls what users may add but does not register any marketplaces. To both restrict and pre-register a marketplace for all users, set both in `managed-settings.json`:
853	
854	```json theme={null}
855	{
856	  "strictKnownMarketplaces": [
857	    { "source": "github", "repo": "acme-corp/plugins" }
858	  ],
859	  "extraKnownMarketplaces": {
860	    "acme-tools": {
861	      "source": { "source": "github", "repo": "acme-corp/plugins" }
862	    }
863	  }
864	}
865	```
866	
867	With only `strictKnownMarketplaces` set, users can still add the allowed marketplace manually via `/plugin marketplace add`, but it is not available automatically.
868	
869	**Important notes**:
870	
871	* Restrictions are checked BEFORE any network requests or filesystem operations
872	* When blocked, users see clear error messages indicating the source is blocked by managed policy
873	* The restriction applies only to adding NEW marketplaces; previously installed marketplaces remain accessible
874	* Managed settings have the highest precedence and cannot be overridden
875	
876	See [Managed marketplace restrictions](/en/plugin-marketplaces#managed-marketplace-restrictions) for user-facing documentation.
877	
878	### Managing plugins
879	
880	Use the `/plugin` command to manage plugins interactively:
881	
882	* Browse available plugins from marketplaces
883	* Install/uninstall plugins
884	* Enable/disable plugins
885	* View plugin details (skills, agents, hooks provided)
886	* Add/remove marketplaces
887	
888	Learn more about the plugin system in the [plugins documentation](/en/plugins).
889	
890	## Environment variables
891	
892	Environment variables let you control Claude Code behavior without editing settings files. Any variable can also be configured in [`settings.json`](#available-settings) under the `env` key to apply it to every session or roll it out to your team.
893	
894	See the [environment variables reference](/en/env-vars) for the full list.
895	
896	## Tools available to Claude
897	
898	Claude Code has access to a set of tools for reading, editing, searching, running commands, and orchestrating subagents. Tool names are the exact strings you use in permission rules and hook matchers.
899	
900	See the [tools reference](/en/tools-reference) for the full list and Bash tool behavior details.
901	
902	## See also
903	
904	* [Permissions](/en/permissions): permission system, rule syntax, tool-specific patterns, and managed policies
905	* [Authentication](/en/authentication): set up user access to Claude Code
906	* [Troubleshooting](/en/troubleshooting): solutions for common configuration issues
907

content.text:
Based on the official Claude Code documentation:

**.claude.json** stores app state and UI preferences:
- Location: `~/.claude.json` (global, user-only)
- Contains: theme, editor mode, OAuth session tokens, per-project trust decisions, personal MCP servers, UI toggles (showTurnDuration, terminalProgressBarEnabled)
- Managed through `/config` interface rather than hand-editing
- Not shared with your team

**settings.json** enforces configuration and behavior:
- Locations: `~/.claude/settings.json` (user), `.claude/settings.json` (project, shared), `.claude/settings.local.json` (personal project overrides)
- Contains: permissions, hooks, environment variables, model selection, status line config
- Same JSON format across all scopes, with precedence hierarchy (managed > local > project > user)
- Project-level files are committed to git and shared with teammates

**Use settings.json for** team policies, permissions, hooks, and consistent behavior across sessions. **Use .claude.json** for personal preferences that don't affect behavior - Claude Code manages most of it automatically.
